sentences.text: There were about 16 youngsters from the Hashomer Hatzair organization.

And there were also many youngsters who volunteer, I mean youngsters-- the ages were between 19 and 25, that were connected with the families of the Hashomer Hatzair or were in bunkers hiding already or they were in certain industries that were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] employing Jews in Warsaw.
I don't know if you are aware, but there was about 36,000 Jews in Warsaw in January and February and March, until the real uprising.
They were working for Germany industry, like Tobbens, Schultz, and-- these were places which were making brooms, uniforms, and the others.
Precinct 1 one that was on Franciszkanska Street was Antek Zuckerman in command.
And the communication of the-- between these places was not done by any telephone or telegram, but mouth-to-mouth.
And they were using messengers, people who could rely on and delivering messages as they could, [INAUDIBLE] best knowledge.
And they had to know the routes, over the rooftops, underneath the ruins, and so on.
I participated in the messenger services between Antek Zuckerman bunker and Mila 18 of Mordechai Anielewicz.
And--
With the operation of the--

The minute we have seen that there's no opposition from the Germans, we directed the people to the gates of the homes and helping them carry whatever they had with them to make it as smooth operation as possible.
And once they got into homes, we knew the Germans would not follow them to recover any number of people.
There were no small children or anything.
They were mostly adults in the ages of 25 to 35, mostly women.
And the reason that they decided to go to Umschlagplatz, because the conditions of living in Warsaw ghetto was extremely difficult, for many reasons.
Now, the--
--operation--
Today?
It was devastated.
First of all, it was much smaller.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The previous ghetto was all the way to Leszno Street, the-- the second ghetto reduction.

But after 1942, when there was a total evacuation of Jews-- I mean deportation of Jews from Warsaw ghetto, the Germans minimized the size of the ghetto from Leszno to Gesia Street and then to Niska Street and from Okopowa to-- to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Muranow and is the garden of Krasinski.
At the gate-- at the garden of Krasinski was a gate, entrance gate to this ghetto.

And at that time, some of the Germans were trying to lure Antek Zuckerman into negotiation of surrendering to the Germans on the conditions recognized by the international law, by coming out with a white flag.

However, they were met with shots from the Franciszkanska Street ghetto-- I mean factory of Antek Zuckerman, who refused to meet with any Germans participating in negotiation of evacuation of any people from Warsaw.
No.
We were told by the-- by the people who were coming from-- from the bunkers of the order bunkers and headquarters about this event.
And we knew that-- that these were quite reliable people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now in our weapons, we had small Molotov cocktail.
We didn't know at the time that they were calling this Molotov cocktail.
But they were loaded bottles with little sticking-out knot, and just to be ignited and thrown.
We used them during the period of time when the 360 people were being taken apart, when we noticed that there were approaching vehicles from the Gesia Street [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gate.
And somehow we succeeded to stop them.
And this was perhaps one of the foremost successful operation against the Germans of trying to save 300 people from devastation and deportation to [INAUDIBLE].
I was directing the people to the hiding places prepared for them [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] during the operation and after the operation.
I also participated in the messenger services for Anielewicz and Antek Zuckerman.
Antek.
Yeah.
He was a-- a really Polish-looking fellow, very excitable and-- but totally devoted to [INAUDIBLE] causes, how to succeed of this operation and get the upper hand of it.
Really looking realistically of chances to see if we-- how long can we prevail in fighting the Germans' ghetto?
Anielewicz was perhaps more calm than Antek Zuckerman, and of great sophistication than Antek Zuckerman ever had, because of this-- of his educational background.
He was attending one of the gymnasiums in Warsaw.
Never-- I never have seen my mother after I--
No.
There was no way of finding out.
There was nobody in the-- the place where we were living, so I presume that she was taken with the deportation of the Warsaw, the first waves of July of 1942.
Where they were taken, it's very hard to tell, but most likely to Treblinka.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other things that I remember from fighting the Warsaw Ghetto, that there were many youngster, I mean really children, the age of 12 and 14, that somehow managed when the fighting started [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on April 19, 1943, and the Germans rolled in the tanks--
There were calm-- mostly calm situation.
There was sporadic shooting and devastation of ghetto by the German artillery.

They wanted to devastate as many homes, as many houses as they could without-- because they couldn't get response from the Jewish community, from the Jewish people in Warsaw Ghetto, to cooperate of-- for the-- for the deportation.
And none of the units wanted to get into ghetto and plunder or look for things.
So they simply were devastating it [INAUDIBLE].

In the meantime, they were-- we were training people and whatever help we were getting from the outside of the Polish people with whom we made-- contact was made to get the weapons for fighting and so on.
There was just a drop in the bucket.
Through the sewer lines.
No.
After I came to ghetto through the sewer lines, I never went through the sewer lines again.
However, I believe that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Vladka Meed and Ben Meed, who were present in the Warsaw ghetto uprising, were-- got out of the ghetto through the sewer lines.
And that was not a simple encounter, as I described.
You had to be totally prepared and know the layout of the sewer lines to know where to get out.
If you were staying for a long period of time, you were absorbed by the gasses and you couldn't survive under no condition.
There were many cases that people were looking for the sewer line hoping to escape, but could not survive without proper orientation and total preparation.
Depressed.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was depression, definitely.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the spirit of being able to fight for the life was perhaps greater or greater value than-- to them at that time than just giving up and marching to the Umschlagplatz.
There was-- one of the Anielewicz letter's messages was that it was-- one of the sweetest things in my life was to be able to fight as a free Jew for freedom.
I think this message is written in [NON-ENGLISH].
No.
I did not.
This message was sent [INAUDIBLE] to-- [DRINKS].
This message was sent to Antek Zuckerman just before the uprising ceased.

I was in his bunker quite often and there were other people, [INAUDIBLE] and some of the leaders of the-- of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of the leaders who had men, smaller groups to deal with-- to protect the-- certain points of the uprising in Warsaw.
They were placed in such way that they could be easily heard or deliver the message.
Messages were not written.
There was very little bit of written messages sent, so people had to deliver it by mouth and precisely delivering what they wanted to say and how to say it, to who to say it.
They had to know the people and describe the people and know the routes over the-- in the houses, over the attics, over the roofs, back into-- into streets and then under rubbles of houses.
Sometime we-- across the Nalewki Street we build a so-called bridge.
That was actually not a bridge, but a wall of stones and debris to be able to crawl at-- during the night when the Germans couldn't see us passing to the other side of the street.

And on one side, there was the-- part of-- of Nalewki Street that you could crawl to [INAUDIBLE] Street and the other side was the street that was beginning of Franciszkanska Street and the headquarter of Antek Zuckerman.

If there were shots that I heard, I don't remember, because there was constantly shooting by Germans, even at night, just to make sure that there is everything-- you know, in the evening, there was quiet.
There was not a quiet.
Germans were shooting constantly.
I would like to mention to you that they ignited number of houses.
And the ghetto started to slowly burn.
And Polish people were seeing this.
And they were saying, this is the end of the Jewish community in Warsaw.

However, when the uprising started on the April 19th, this was a different story, because they knew that-- they heard the shots and they knew the Germans exaggerated quite a bit by saying that in a day or two, the ghetto will fall.
The ghetto did not fall until some six weeks.
I was at first of the bunker of Anielewicz.
Then I was at the bunker of [INAUDIBLE] Street 4.
This was one of the bunkers that we were using on the way to Nalewki Street, then Antek Zuckerman bunkers.
This was like a stopover or if necessary asking for help.
Or even there was no need for any help to obtain, we continue without exposing any evidence that there is a bunker.
We had to be very, very careful with this situation, because they were always another pair of eyes that were seeing.
And for the sake of [INAUDIBLE], trying to save the life.
I was on my way to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with one of the messages from Anielewicz to Antek Zuckerman.
And when I heard the fighting and the rumbling tanks- and-- then I knew that we have to put up quite a bit of effort.
I returned the Mila 18 headquarter.

Mila 18 headquarter was quite well prepared for-- with many things, including some food, to resist for some period of time, until there was a-- this what people see today with the monument to the bunker was a very nice building standing with all facilities intact, prepared for absorption of people.
And it was all shattered to pieces by the German artillery and-- and the air force.
They were using air force to-- to devastate the ghetto.
My main thing was to-- at night to prepare Molotov cocktails out of any little cans or-- or bottles, and to gather the material for it, and show the other people how to prepare it.
And the other thing was delivering messages from bunker to bunker.
Very simply.
You-- you had a wick.
And the wick we prepared out of cotton mixed with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm losing my word.
I am-- the wick was mixed with threads of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do you call this?
Plumbers are using this for-- palm-- palm string.
And also using-- using burlap up and twisting it into thread until it became really soakable.

Then we just left a little bit out of it with-- at the opening of the bottle, we placed [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] paraffin-- paraffin is substance of use for candles-- and very gently opening it, so the gasoline can be soaked up and be affected by the-- by the heat of the wick.

You had to make your choice of the proper material for this, since it was of great importance that a wick will be ignited at the time when it hits and explodes, that the wick is lit and stays lit with a little bit of soaking that penetrates from the bottle.
All depends how close was the enemy.
But there were sporadic cases that kids-- how do they got to this equipment and jumped off the balconies under-- underneath the tanks to explode them.
There was a sight that only people who were actually witnesses could-- could describe it.
On one side, you wanted to scream out of glory for-- for such thing.
On the other side, you knew another youngster, innocent youngster, just sacrificed his life for this, saving some-- some other people.
Yes.
I did see this on Zamenhof Street.
Yes.
There was a-- a tank was entering the ghetto through the Gesia Street.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] SS men had a tank and open dome.
And a youngster jumped out out of a second- or third-store balcony with a lit bottle-- cocktail-- Molotov cocktail and just happened that he fell in front of the tank.
And the tank crushed him.
The chains of the thing-- of the tank crushed him.
But he succeeded to-- the bottle-- the Molotov cocktail succeeded to explode and explode the tank.
There were three Germans that jump out of the tank when they have seen it.
And they were-- the uniforms were afire.
And what happened to them, I don't really remember, didn't-- didn't care.
But that was a glorious moment to see how a-- a child could sacrifice his life for the sake of exploding such tanks.
Yes.
That was most likely on the fifth day of the-- when the fighting began.
Fifth day-- oh, third day of the fighting, when it began.
In other words, by the-- on 22nd of April.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
We used to call this in Polish [POLISH], means 'bottle of gas.'
A bomb?
Pardon me.
Somehow we had-- we had some gasoline and we had naphtha.
Naphtha was a-- a byproduct of oil that was a cooking-- for cooking purposes.
And there was quite a little bit available for it.
And in every bunker they had canisters of such material for cooking purposes and for creating-- building, making bombs.
No.
Yes.
I had a-- we-- I-- I was given a gun that I would say did not fire quite well, because I couldn't tell if I really hit anybody or-- and-- but I was aiming quite well.
And I was sure that it will come to good use.
The ammunition was made by a girl sitting in a-- in the bunkers and preparing, pouring-- pouring lead into a certain form.
The lead, we had quite a bit of it from previously [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] collected by the leaders of the preparation for the uprising.
I would like to mention to you that during the January 6, there were some girls with us.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of them, [?
Hella, ?] had a gun in her hand.
And the Germans were all screaming, they have-- Jews have arms and women are shooting at us.
It's sounded in German that-- like this, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

And it was a sound that we are very, very proud of, because we couldn't imagine that Germans could actually recognize such things as the fact that the women are fighting alongside of us, just as the children, they were fighting with us.
And--
When they were hit in the helmets and they dropped the guns and they raised their hands and asking for-- for mercy.
That's when they started to scream, the Jews, Jews have the weapons and the women are-- are shooting at them.

And now we're coming back to this incident of Gesia Street and Zamenhof Street, when the youngsters explode this tank and that tank and immobilize-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was not another thing following it.
And I remember that we were waiting and seeing, trying to see what actually will happen to it.
And then we-- they came during the night.
It was pitch dark and there were no lights involved.
That-- they pulled the tank out-- out of the ghetto.
And what they had done with it, I don't know.
But it must have been of some value to them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a [INAUDIBLE] feeling to see the turret of the tank open and the gun hanging with the nose down.
And I didn't see how they pulled it out, but I believe they did, because the following day I didn't see any of the thing-- of the parts of tank in the ghetto.
However, what we are very much concerned is that little kid that was-- jumped on the tank.
We couldn't find the body of the child either.

If there is any positive things of the thing, how did we survive, well, on the way to the bunker of-- of Antek Zuckerman, I had to enter the bunker on [INAUDIBLE] 4, because the shots of the German approaching were too close.
And yeah-- I asked for help to enter the bunker.
About-- that was on May the 5th that the Germans somehow got to our bunker and I was in there.
And they threw in tear gas.
And we start choking and we're gasping for air.
And finally, they said to us, we will not shoot at you.
Just raise your hands above your head and come out with the weapons up.
And people started to leave the bunker, because it was impossible to-- there was no breathing air whatsoever.
And when they-- when we got out of the bunker-- and then these people somehow had two little children, five and seven, with them.
And I don't know how did they ever survive all this time.
But the Germans send in-- after we all got out, the Germans sent in two dogs.
And the dogs didn't get out of the bunker.
The gas affected them, the dogs, and they-- they died.
